title: Efficient Quantum Fourier Transforms For Semisimple Algebras
authors: Ben Foxman, Barak Nehoran, Yongshan Ding
abstract: The quantum Fourier transform (QFT) is a fundamental primitive in quantum computation and quantum information. In this work, we generalize the QFT for finite groups to a QFT for finite-dimensional semisimple algebras, and give efficient quantum Fourier transforms for the partition algebra $P_n(d)$, Brauer algebra $B_n(d)$, and walled Brauer algebra $B_{r,s}(d)$. These algebras play important roles in generalized Schur-Weyl duality, statistical physics and many-body systems, and have recently found several applications in quantum algorithms.   Unlike the group case, the Fourier transform over a semisimple algebra can be non-unitary. Nevertheless, we show that when the parameter $d$ is sufficiently large, the Fourier transform is well approximated by a unitary operator. Furthermore, we show that for each of the algebras $A$ from above, such an approximate Fourier transform can be implemented efficiently: we give a quantum algorithm with gate complexity $\mathrm{poly}(n,\log d,\log(1/\varepsilon))$ for approximating the Fourier transform to error $(d^{-1/2} + \varepsilon) \cdot \mathrm{poly}(|A|)$. Along the way, we establish several properties of the Fourier basis of semisimple algebras that may be of independent interest.
summary: The paper presents an efficient quantum algorithm to implement a Quantum Fourier Transform for a class of semisimple diagram algebras. By using a unitary approximation valid in the large-d limit, the authors overcome the non-unitarity of these algebras, offering a path toward new quantum algorithms for problems involving Schur-Weyl duality and statistical mechanics.
main_problem: Generalizing the Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) from finite groups to finite-dimensional semisimple algebras, specifically addressing the challenges of non-unitarity and non-orthogonality in diagram algebras.
main_result: The development of an efficient quantum algorithm with polylogarithmic gate complexity that provides a unitary approximation of the QFT for partition, Brauer, and walled Braver algebras.
method: A recursive approach using a modified separation of variables (double transversal factorization) and unitary embeddings to approximate the non-unitary Fourier transform.
model_or_system: Finite-dimensional semisimple diagram algebras, including the Partition algebra P_n(d), Brauer algebra B_n(d), and walled Brauer algebra B_{r,s}(d), which are linked to Schur-Weyl duality.
key_observables: Gate complexity, error bounds (in terms of d and epsilon), and the preservation of the propagating number in diagrams.
important_parameters: The dimension parameter d (requiring large d for unitarity), the algebra rank n, and the error tolerance epsilon.
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes d is sufficiently large to ensure the Fourier basis is approximately orthonormal and that certain alternate embeddings can be implemented unitarily.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows diagram multiplication in the symmetric group; Figure 2 illustrates basis elements for partition, Brauer, and walled Brauer algebras; Figure 3 shows the block-diagonalization of the regular representation; Figure 4 shows the action of algebra generators on graphs.
paper_structure: The paper covers background and definitions, establishes the delta-nice property of algebras, details the QFT algorithm for diagram algebras, discusses subalgebra adapted bases, and provides formal proofs for complexity and path extension isometries.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful new algorithmic primitive (QFT for semisimple algebras) that could potentially generalize the Hidden Subgroup Problem and enable efficient computation of complex representation-theoretic multiplicities used in many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Diagram multiplication in the symmetric group algebra.
Figure 2: Example elements of the partition algebra 𝑃𝑛(𝑑), Brauer algebra 𝐵𝑛(𝑑), and the walled Brauer algebra 𝐵𝑟,𝑠(𝑑). See Section 4.2 for formal definitions of each of these algebras.

Figure 3: The Fourier transform block-diagonalizes the natural 𝐺-action, known as the regular representation of 𝐺. The |𝜌⟩label specifies an irreducible representation, the |𝑖⟩register denotes a particular copy of 𝜌, and the | 𝑗⟩register is a basis vector within the irreducible subspace indexed by (𝜌, 𝑖).

Figure 4: Actions of the partition algebra generators on a graph 𝐺. The generator 𝑏𝑖contracts the vertices containing labels 𝑖and 𝑖+ 1, merging their label sets. The generator 𝑝𝑖removes the label 𝑖from its current vertex and places it on a new isolated singleton vertex. If 𝑖belongs to a vertex carrying multiple labels, only the label 𝑖is reset; the remaining labels stay on the original vertex.
Figure 5: An example of multiplying two partition diagrams.
